metadata.title: Content, Contribution, and Knowledge Consumption: Uncovering Hidden Topic Structure and Rhetorical Signals in Scientific Texts

abstract: Knowledge production and scientific discourse are observable in published scholarly texts. Citations capture knowledge consumption and impact. Drawing from the sociology of science, our theoretical framework posits scientific communities as thought collectives with distinctive thought styles that embed a hidden topic structure and rhetorical signals into a journal's published articles. We hypothesize and uncover how an article's topic attributes (structure, focus, and newness) and rhetorical attributes (inclusiveness, exclusiveness, tentativeness, and certainty) are related to future knowledge consumption. We empirically test our ideas by applying text mining algorithms to model topics and extract rhetorical signals from 1,646 strategy articles composed of nearly 18 million words generating 172,237 citations over 35 years. We find that strategy articles' hidden topic structure explains 14% of variance in scientific impact. We also show that topic focus and topic newness each independently, directly, and significantly increase impact. As for newness, the first two articles published on a new topic each generate a citation premium >100%, which is higher within the focal thought collective than outside. Importantly, we find that the citation premium of newness increases with greater topic focus Acknowledgments: We thank the editor William P. Wan and two anonymous reviewers for their constructive feedback throughout the review process. We are also grateful to our colleagues (Oregon State

paragraphs: theorizing about and analyzing two understudied aspects of written scientific texts: topic attributes, characterizing "what one says," and rhetorical attributes, signaling "when is it a contribution" (Baldi, 1998;Barrett & Walsham, 2004;A. Kaplan, 1964). Our core premise is that scientific fields are "word systems" created and maintained by their members (Astley, 1985: 499). We observe that words commonly associated with the same topic tend to cluster within the full text of the same article and persist across articles published on this topic in the same journal (Leydesdroff, 1989). For example, the words strategy, alliances, partnerships, and joint ventures repeatedly cluster in published management research articles focused on the topic of strategic alliances. We further observe that certain words often appear to signal authors' intended contributions, not only in an article's abstract, but throughout the entire text (Barrett & Walsham, 2004). For instance, using the word unlike (i.e., "Unlike previous research, this paper . . .") in an article may differentiate the authors' claims by excluding other claims, whereas using the word integrate (i.e., "We integrate three separate research streams . . .") may synthesize the authors' claims by including other claims. Thus, certain words may relate to specific topics (Blei, 2012) or types of rhetoric (Tausczik & Pennebaker, 2010) found in texts.

Building on our core premise of scientific fields as "word systems," we define several essential concepts. The content of scientific articles is the subject matter of the written text, and the contribution of these articles is constructed with claims made within the text (Mone & McKinley, 1993;Weick, 1995;Whetten, 1989). A claim is a statement of assumption, assertion, observation, or evidence. Topics represent the thematic configuration of content within an article and across the set of articles published in a journal. Rhetoric is the argumentation used to support the claims and present the contribution of an article. As we elaborate in our theory and hypotheses, we posit that the collective thinking of the members of a scientific community or "thought collective" imprints a distinctive "thought style" on the articles published by that community as the output of discourse (Fleck, 1979). This "thought style" embeds a topic structure and rhetorical signals into scientific texts. We further posit that identifiable attributes of an article's topics and rhetoric are meaningful predictors of the future consumption and citation of the content and contribution contained in the article's text.

We organize our exploration of topic and rhetorical attributes in five main steps. First, we articulate a conceptual framework in which scientific texts are socially constructed and imbued with a detectable topic structure and rhetorical signals embodied in the words used (Fleck, 1979). Second, we propose salient topic attributes defined in relation to an article's focus (relative concentration of topics) and newness (relative order of entry into topics). We propose key rhetorical attributes (inclusiveness, exclusiveness, tentativeness, and certainty) based on the words typically used to credibly connect claims and establish contribution in scientific texts. Third, we develop a set of hypotheses on how the topic and rhetorical attributes of a scholarly article relate to its subsequent citations. Fourth, we develop a generalizable, scalable methodology for empirically analyzing the content, contribution, and consumption of any article within any scholarly journal's defined corpus. Our toolkit includes automated text analysis techniques and associated measures developed to extract topic and rhetorical attributes from large text corpora. Fifth and finally, we test our hypotheses using the full texts of all 1,646 research articles published in the Strategic Management Journal (SMJ;1980-2014) as our corpus. These articles consist of nearly 18 million words of written text. The use of SMJ as our corpus is especially relevant because our data, based on the Web

to increase citations (Podsakoff et al., 2008). Evidence about the effect of an author's total prior publications is mixed. Some studies suggest a positive influence, while others suggest a negative influence, based on the idea that having fewer but more-often cited prior articles is a reasonable indicator of the likely impact of the author's focal article (Bergh, Perry, & Hanke, 2006).

Reference attributes. Multiple studies find that an article's number of cited references is positively associated with its subsequent impact (Judge et al., 2007;Mingers & Xu, 2010;Phelan et al., 2002). Citation analyses find that management research is becoming more interdisciplinary and is exchanging knowledge flows with diverse fields (Nerur et al., 2016;Rafols et al., 2012).

Journal attributes. Previous work indicates that journal quality seems to have a sizable and significant positive influence on knowledge consumption (Judge et al., 2007;Mingers & Xu, 2010). The top two quartiles of management journals account for 80% of all citations, and their average citation counts per article appear to be quite stable over time (Podsakoff et al., 2005).

Linguistic attributes. Prior work suggests that at a basic level, article length has a positive effect on scientific impact, the number of keywords has a negative effect, and the title length has no effect (Stremersch et al., 2007). Evidence on the impact of greater writing clarity and improved readability is mixed. Some studies find a positive effect (Judge et al., 2007), while others find a negative effect. This leads to the surprising implication that some scientists do not necessarily perceive more readable research to be of higher quality (Stremersch et al., 2007).

Topic attributes. Earlier work reveals interesting initial findings on topic attributes and knowledge consumption (Table 1), which we aim to extend. Published articles may generate citations if utilized by scientists as building blocks in future knowledge production. Forward citations may reflect engagement with the substance of an article's research topic(s) (Baldi, 1998;N. Kaplan, 1965). Prior studies find that the number of other articles within the same journal that are associated with the same topic as a focal article may also positively influence the focal article's forward citations (Bergh et al., 2006). In addition, salient and memorable topics seem to attract attention and generate citations (Colquitt & Zapata-Phelan, 2007). The entry point and positioning of an article's research topic(s) in comparison with the journal's full set of topics also appears to affect the article's citations (Shafique, 2013). The importance of topic focus and newness is supported by previous findings showing that "intellectual content" influences citations (Baldi, 1998: 829) and "novelty is prized in the literature" (Davis, 2015: 181).

Some studies suggest a positive influence, while others suggest a negative influence, based on the idea that having fewer but more-often cited prior articles is a reasonable indicator of the likely impact of the author's focal article (Bergh, Perry, & Hanke, 2006).

Reference attributes. Multiple studies find that an article's number of cited references is positively associated with its subsequent impact (Judge et al., 2007;Mingers & Xu, 2010;Phelan et al., 2002). Citation analyses find that management research is becoming more interdisciplinary and is exchanging knowledge flows with diverse fields (Nerur et al., 2016;Rafols et al., 2012).

Journal attributes. Previous work indicates that journal quality seems to have a sizable and significant positive influence on knowledge consumption (Judge et al., 2007;Mingers & Xu, 2010). The top two quartiles of management journals account for 80% of all citations, and their average citation counts per article appear to be quite stable over time (Podsakoff et al., 2005).

Linguistic attributes. Prior work suggests that at a basic level, article length has a positive effect on scientific impact, the number of keywords has a negative effect, and the title length has no effect (Stremersch et al., 2007). Evidence on the impact of greater writing clarity and improved readability is mixed. Some studies find a positive effect (Judge et al., 2007), while others find a negative effect. This leads to the surprising implication that some scientists do not necessarily perceive more readable research to be of higher quality (Stremersch et al., 2007).

Topic attributes. Earlier work reveals interesting initial findings on topic attributes and knowledge consumption (Table 1), which we aim to extend. Published articles may generate citations if utilized by scientists as building blocks in future knowledge production. Forward citations may reflect engagement with the substance of an article's research topic(s) (Baldi, 1998;N. Kaplan, 1965). Prior studies find that the number of other articles within the same journal that are associated with the same topic as a focal article may also positively influence the focal article's forward citations (Bergh et al., 2006). In addition, salient and memorable topics seem to attract attention and generate citations (Colquitt & Zapata-Phelan, 2007). The entry point and positioning of an article's research topic(s) in comparison with the journal's full set of topics also appears to affect the article's citations (Shafique, 2013). The importance of topic focus and newness is supported by previous findings showing that "intellectual content" influences citations (Baldi, 1998: 829) and "novelty is prized in the literature" (Davis, 2015: 181).

paragraphs: A related research stream investigates rhetorical practices that establish contribution by employing specific words to connect claims intra-and intertextually (Alvesson & Sandberg, 2011;Baldi, 1998;Barrett & Walsham, 2004;Johnson, 2003;Locke & Golden-Biddle, 1997;Mercer & Di Marco, 2003). Rhetorical structure theory (Mann & Thompson, 1988) posits that linked claims are detectable via connective discourse markers (i.e., words including and, but, also, and moreover). Studies on language use in context suggest that such markers (1) signal the presence of a particular relationship between terms and (2) are genre specific, implying that not all relations are signaled in all genres (Taboada, 2006).

In the management research genre, Locke and Golden-Biddle (1997) identify rhetorical practices that appear to confer credibility on contributions. Management articles typically use a mixture of connective discourse markers to specify the intertextual coherence of an article and problematize its context (Alvesson & Sandberg, 2011). Particularly prominent among these markers are indicators of rhetorical inclusiveness, exclusiveness, tentativeness, and certainty (Tausczik & Pennebaker, 2010). For example, within the same article, authors may make a range of inclusive (i.e., and, both, not only) and exclusive (i.e., but, else, nor) claims (Pennebaker, Booth, Boyd, & Francis, 2015). These claims may credibly uphold, challenge, or even negate a field's assumptions and reinforce an article's contribution, thereby driving consumption (Alvesson & Sandberg, 2011). Authors may also credibly express their claims with varying degrees of tentativeness (i.e., maybe or perhaps) and certainty (i.e., always or never; Locke & Golden-Biddle, 1997). Readers might find articles exhibiting a blend of these rhetorical attributes credible and cite their respective contributions in future work.

In sum, our literature review (Table 1) suggests that recent studies of the drivers of scientific impact tend to underutilize topic and rhetorical attributes as major constructs. Instead, these attributes typically serve as minor control variables in their respective research designs, if included at all. We offer two possible explanations for the dearth of studies in this space. The first may be the lack of a cogent theory explaining how collective knowledge production infuses scholarly articles with perceptible topic and rhetorical attributes and why these attributes affect knowledge consumption. The second may be the lack of an efficient and scalable general methodology for precisely detecting these attributes and quantitatively testing their effects for entire journals and research domains. Now, we address the first possibility by conceptualizing how scientific discourse affects content, contribution, and knowledge consumption. Later, we address the second possibility by using automated text analysis techniques to extract topic and rhetorical attributes from published scientific texts.

sections.header_raw: Conceptual Framework: Thought Collectives and Thought Styles
sections.header_norm: conceptual framework thought collectives and thought styles
paragraphs: Our proposed conceptual framework draws on the theories of Ludwik Fleck, who is widely regarded to be the "founder of [the] sociology of science" (Latour, 2005: 112). Fleck (1979) is the originator of the concepts of thought collectives and thought styles and is the first to theorize about their possible effects on scientific knowledge production and consumption. Fleck's central thesis is that scientific discovery is a collective process undertaken by thought collectives and imbued with thought styles (Harwood, 1986). A thought collective is a "community of scientists working on the basis of joint convictions as to which knowledge shall be considered proved, which methodologies are scientifically valid, and which criteria of scientificity are acceptable" (Schnelle, 1981: 733). The community's shared "unarticulated convictions" are the collective's thought style (Schnelle, 1981). Individual scientists may be members of multiple thought collectives (Fleck, 1979) and may thereby be influenced by the circulation of ideas among various thought collectives and thought styles (Mößner, 2011).

By publishing texts and organizing conferences, thought collectives such as scientific societies and disciplinary associations "identify specific areas of inquiry as their own and provide opportunities for like-minded groups of individuals to coordinate research agendas" (Huff, 2009: 288). The idea of thought collectives appears congruent with prior studies of scientific discourse in the field of management. For instance, as Astley and Zammuto (1992: 448) describe, "like all scientific fields, organization science is a language system created and maintained by a community of scholars engaged in a sense-making exercise as they invent conceptual terminology and construct theoretical meanings." A thought style is "characterized by common features in the problems of interest to a thought collective, by the judgements which the thought collective consider evident, and by the methods which it applies as means of cognition" (Mößner, 2011: 365). The idea of thought styles is often implicit in studies of scientific discourse in management. For example, as Weick (1995: 286) states regarding research contributions, "an innovation is assumption specific . . . appreciated only by those who share the same initial assumptions." Thought styles exhibit unique technical and literary features reflected in the words used by members of the thought collective to express their ideas (Mößner, 2011). As Fleck (1979: 109) explains, "all words bear a more or less distinctive coloring conforming to a given thought style, a character which changes during their passage from one collective to the next."

In line with our core premise that scientific fields are "word systems" (Astley, 1985: 499), we posit that a thought collective's thought style is latent but discernible (Fleck, 1979). The thought style is latent since it is embedded in the words that codify the essential ideas in the scientific texts published in the collective's journal(s) (Astley & Zammuto, 1992). However, the thought style remains discernible because a journal's contributing authors, editorial team, and reviewers, who are all part of the thought collective, establish a "cognitive consensus" (Vinkler, 1994: 496). This shared understanding about the meaning of an article's words emerges via the review and publication process and enables the authors to compose the content of the article's topics and credibly connect claims using rhetoric that signals a contribution. Thus, we contend that a thought collective's distinctive thought style affects the topic structure and the rhetorical signals appearing in the written text of a journal's published articles.

sections.paper_id: Antons et al. - 2019 - Content, Contribution, and Knowledge Consumption Uncovering Hidden Topic Structure and Rhetorical S
paragraphs: Applying our framework, we first define topic structure and formulate a baseline hypothesis. We then develop our two main topic attributes (focus and newness) and our four main rhetorical attributes (inclusiveness, exclusiveness, tentativeness, and certainty). The observable scientific output of a thought collective is scholarly articles published in the journal(s) affiliated with the society or association representing the collective's membership. The topics and rhetoric in published journal articles embody the characteristic thought style of the collective. Each published article in a scholarly journal's corpus features a unique set of thoughts arranged as a collection of words, organized into an identifiable cluster of research topics, and expressed with a combination of rhetoric. We define topic structure as the relative distribution of distinguishable research topics in a given article as compared with the entire underlying distribution of hidden or latent topics embodied in a scholarly journal's defined corpus. In essence, an article's topic structure is a snapshot of the word choices representing the thought collective's encoding of its signature thought style into an article's text. Note that a journal's overall topic landscape emerges from the words appearing in the full written text of every article published in the journal over time and not just the title, abstract, and keywords. The word choices in an article capture its intellectual substance, which may be consumed to differing degrees in subsequent research produced in one or more thought collectives. Therefore, as a baseline hypothesis, H0, we propose that an article's topic structure affects its scientific impact: H0: The distinct research topics within a journal article's topic structure (relative distribution of latent topics) differ in their scientific impact.

Building on this baseline hypothesis and the idea that every journal article has a topic structure that represents its relative distribution of latent research topics, we define topic focus. Depending on the research question, disciplinary standards, applicable methods, and journal's scope and degree of specialization, as well as the scholarly aims of the authors, reviewers, and editors involved, some articles may exclusively cover one major research topic, while others may span several topics. Most articles combine two or more research topics, which may be balanced or unbalanced in terms of the amount of weighting or level of emphasis that each topic has, as compared with all of the topics in the article. The more content in an article that is directly associated with a given topic, the greater the importance of that specific topic to the article.

We define topic focus as the relative concentration of topics in an article's topic structure. An article's degree of topic focus captures the extent to which a given article's combination of topics is concentrated or diffuse. Highly concentrated content is often easier to extract and apply than highly diffuse content (Weick & Roberts, 1993). Once detected, concentrated knowledge is also typically more readily conveyed to others (Galunic & Rodan, 1998). Articles with greater topic focus may initially be more likely to attract a thought collective's attention (Fagan, 2009), then become strongly linked with a given topic (Bergh et al., 2006), and eventually emerge as an often-cited source for a well-defined topic (Leydesdroff, 1989). Using this logic, we hypothesize that articles with more concentrated knowledge content are more likely to be cited: H1: The greater a journal article's degree of topic focus (relative concentration of topics), the greater its scientific impact.

We expect that as more articles are published in a journal over time, as editorial policies change, or as scientific fields evolve, new research topics may emerge through the continuous circulation of ideas within a thought collective and across collectives (Fleck, 1979). Hence, we define topic newness as the relative order of entry in which a given article is associated with the latent research topics embodied in a scholarly journal's corpus. The earlier in an emergent topic's life span that a given article is associated with a particular topic, the greater its newness. Note that a single article may exhibit newness or fresh thinking in multiple topic areas. Further note that an article may not necessarily exhibit newness in the topics with which it is the most directly associated. Newness, or the timing of the introduction of the topic within the collective, is a critical aspect of novelty. As "novelty is prized in the literature" (Davis, 2015: 181), articles featuring salient and memorable topics appear to attract the attention of scientists engaged in knowledge production, which may generate citations (Colquitt & Zapata-Phelan, 2007). A recent empirical analysis of 17.9 million papers spanning all scientific fields supports this view and suggests that "papers with an injection of novelty into an otherwise exceptionally familiar mass of prior work are unusually likely to have high impact" (Uzzi, Mukherjee, Stringer, & Jones, 2013: 470). The order of entry and comparative positioning of an article's research topics in relation to a journal's spectrum of latent topics also appear to affect the article's forward citations, especially as the journal's topic structure changes over time (Shafique, 2013). Among thought collective members, new topics attract interest, (Fagan, 2009), affect idea circulation, and influence thought style evolution (Fleck, 1979), all of which enhance an article's scientific impact. Thus, we propose that articles exhibiting more topic newness are more likely to be cited:
H2: The greater a journal article's degree of topic newness (relative order of entry), the greater its scientific impact.

The rhetorical practices used in journal articles enable authors to establish contribution by signaling the credible claims and relevant context of research ideas (Baldi, 1998;Locke & Golden-Biddle, 1997). An inclusive rhetorical style may be associated with synthesizing the existing literature and showing convergence, progress, consensus, or agreement (Johnson, 2003). In contrast, an exclusive rhetorical style may be associated with differentiating the existing literature and showing divergence, lack of progress, lack of consensus, or disagreement (Barrett & Walsham, 2004). Inclusiveness may reflect the intent to reinforce or reinterpret prior work, while exclusiveness may reflect the intent to challenge or negate prior work. Balancing rhetorical practices reflects hedging, which is "part of the wider system of politeness designed to redress the threat research claims contain to the 'face' of other scientists" (Hyland, 1996: 434). Indeed, in management research, articles often use a mix of inclusive and exclusive argumentation to organize claims and substantiate contributions (Locke & Golden-Biddle, 1997;Weick, 1995).

Rhetorical tentativeness may indicate that the problematizing is indirect, incomplete, inadequate, or incommensurable, while rhetorical certainty may indicate that the problematizing is direct, complete, adequate, and commensurable (Alvesson & Sandberg, 2011;Locke & Golden-Biddle, 1997). A balance of tentativeness and certainty may help authors hedge by appropriately positioning and presenting their own contributions in a written text. "Hedges allow writers to anticipate possible opposition to claims by expressing statements with precision, caution, and diplomatic deference to the views of colleagues" (Hyland, 1996: 433). Note that expressing claims with an extremely high degree of certainty and little tentativeness might appear overly dogmatic. In contrast, expressing claims with an extremely high degree of tentativeness and little certainty might appear overly cautious. Indeed, management research articles frequently incorporate a combination of tentative and certain argumentation, not only in the introduction and conclusion section, but throughout the entire written exposition (Johnson, 2003). We contend that by balancing inclusiveness and exclusiveness or tentativeness and certainty in their rhetoric, authors may spotlight the basis for credibly claiming a contribution. The absolute difference between the levels of inclusiveness and exclusiveness or between the levels of tentativeness and certainty in a journal article is a proxy for the degree of balance maintained by the authors in utilizing these distinct rhetorical styles. Thus, we propose:

H3a: The balance (absolute difference) between inclusive and exclusive rhetoric is positively related to a journal article's scientific impact. H3b: The balance (absolute difference) between tentative and certain rhetoric is positively related to a journal article's scientific impact.

Our earlier logic for H1 explains that more concentrated knowledge content is more likely to be cited because it is easier to identify, access, and communicate (Galunic & Rodan, 1998;Weick & Roberts, 1993). Our rationale for H2 explains that newer knowledge content is more likely to be cited because it is salient and memorable, which attracts attention (Colquitt & Zapata-Phelan, 2007). In essence, concentrated knowledge content is simply more easily absorbed, while newer knowledge content is simply more easily noticed. Recalling our justification for H2, harnessing an admixture of familiar prior work and unfamiliar new work is desirable because scientists often have trouble searching unfamiliar domains and truly new ideas can be difficult to absorb and communicate (Uzzi et al., 2013). Note that diffuse topics dilute an article's focus and impede knowledge absorption by a thought collective, while concentrated topics sharpen focus and enhance absorption (Fagan, 2009). However, if the thought styles of different collectives differ dramatically in their word systems and accepted meanings, then the intercollective exchange of ideas becomes difficult and ideas may end up lost in translation (Mößner, 2011). The more intracollective knowledge content that a scholarly article incorporates from prior research, the more closely aligned the article is likely to be with the focal collective's thought style (Löwy, 1998). The article may be more coherent if it is composed with a familiar set of building blocks from its originating thought collective, rather than an unfamiliar patchwork of ideas imported from various collectives. An already novel article may become more readily detected, understood, and applied in knowledge production and thereby generate more citations. However, articles featuring new topics and employing exclusive rhetoric may position their contributions in a way that competes with or supplants alternatives (Johnson, 2003). H6a: The positive effect of a journal article's degree of topic newness on its scientific impact is amplified by its balance (absolute difference) between inclusive and exclusive rhetoric.

In contrast, articles featuring new topics and incorporating rhetorical certainty may position their contributions by provocatively addressing shortcomings and pointing out what is wrong in the literature (Johnson, 2003). New topics may appear more palatable but remain provocative to a thought collective when expressed with a careful combination of tentative and certain rhetoric (Corley & Wedeking, 2014;Hyland, 1996). Applying our framework, we first define topic structure and formulate a baseline hypothesis. We then develop our two main topic attributes (focus and newness) and our four main rhetorical attributes (inclusiveness, exclusiveness, tentativeness, and certainty). The observable scientific output of a thought collective is scholarly articles published in the journal(s) affiliated with the society or association representing the collective's membership. The topics and rhetoric in published journal articles embody the characteristic thought style of the collective. Each published article in a scholarly journal's corpus features a unique set of thoughts arranged as a collection of words, organized into an identifiable cluster of research topics, and expressed with a combination of rhetoric. We define topic structure as the relative distribution of distinguishable research topics in a given article as compared with the entire underlying distribution of hidden or latent topics embodied in a scholarly journal's defined corpus. In essence, an article's topic structure is a snapshot of the word choices representing the thought collective's encoding of its signature thought style into an article's text. Note that a journal's overall topic landscape emerges from the words appearing in the full written text of every article published in the journal over time and not just the title, abstract, and keywords. The word choices in an article capture its intellectual substance, which may be consumed to differing degrees in subsequent research produced in one or more thought collectives. Therefore, as a baseline hypothesis, H0, we propose that an article's topic structure affects its scientific impact: H0: The distinct research topics within a journal article's topic structure (relative distribution of latent topics) differ in their scientific impact.

Building on this baseline hypothesis and the idea that every journal article has a topic structure that represents its relative distribution of latent research topics, we define topic focus. Depending on the research question, disciplinary standards, applicable methods, and journal's scope and degree of specialization, as well as the scholarly aims of the authors, reviewers, and editors involved, some articles may exclusively cover one major research topic, while others may span several topics. Most articles combine two or more research topics, which may be balanced or unbalanced in terms of the amount of weighting or level of emphasis that each topic has, as compared with all of the topics in the article. The more content in an article that is directly associated with a given topic, the greater the importance of that specific topic to the article.

We define topic focus as the relative concentration of topics in an article's topic structure. An article's degree of topic focus captures the extent to which a given article's combination of topics is concentrated or diffuse. Highly concentrated content is often easier to extract and apply than highly diffuse content (Weick & Roberts, 1993). Once detected, concentrated knowledge is also typically more readily conveyed to others (Galunic & Rodan, 1998). Articles with greater topic focus may initially be more likely to attract a thought collective's attention (Fagan, 2009), then become strongly linked with a given topic (Bergh et al., 2006), and eventually emerge as an often-cited source for a well-defined topic (Leydesdroff, 1989). Using this logic, we hypothesize that articles with more concentrated knowledge content are more likely to be cited: H1: The greater a journal article's degree of topic focus (relative concentration of topics), the greater its scientific impact.

We expect that as more articles are published in a journal over time, as editorial policies change, or as scientific fields evolve, new research topics may emerge through the continuous circulation of ideas within a thought collective and across collectives (Fleck, 1979). Hence, we define topic newness as the relative order of entry in which a given article is associated with the latent research topics embodied in a scholarly journal's corpus. The earlier in an emergent topic's life span that a given article is associated with a particular topic, the greater its newness. Note that a single article may exhibit newness or fresh thinking in multiple topic areas. Further note that an article may not necessarily exhibit newness in the topics with which it is the most directly associated. Newness, or the timing of the introduction of the topic within the collective, is a critical aspect of novelty. As "novelty is prized in the literature" (Davis, 2015: 181), articles featuring salient and memorable topics appear to attract the attention of scientists engaged in knowledge production, which may generate citations (Colquitt & Zapata-Phelan, 2007). A recent empirical analysis of 17.9 million papers spanning all scientific fields supports this view and suggests that "papers with an injection of novelty into an otherwise exceptionally familiar mass of prior work are unusually likely to have high impact" (Uzzi, Mukherjee, Stringer, & Jones, 2013: 470). The order of entry and comparative positioning of an article's research topics in relation to a journal's spectrum of latent topics also appear to affect the article's forward citations, especially as the journal's topic structure changes over time (Shafique, 2013). Among thought collective members, new topics attract interest, (Fagan, 2009), affect idea circulation, and influence thought style evolution (Fleck, 1979), all of which enhance an article's scientific impact. Thus, we propose that articles exhibiting more topic newness are more likely to be cited:

The rhetorical practices used in journal articles enable authors to establish contribution by signaling the credible claims and relevant context of research ideas (Baldi, 1998;Locke & Golden-Biddle, 1997). An inclusive rhetorical style may be associated with synthesizing the existing literature and showing convergence, progress, consensus, or agreement (Johnson, 2003). In contrast, an exclusive rhetorical style may be associated with differentiating the existing literature and showing divergence, lack of progress, lack of consensus, or disagreement (Barrett & Walsham, 2004). Inclusiveness may reflect the intent to reinforce or reinterpret prior work, while exclusiveness may reflect the intent to challenge or negate prior work. Balancing rhetorical practices reflects hedging, which is "part of the wider system of politeness designed to redress the threat research claims contain to the 'face' of other scientists" (Hyland, 1996: 434). Indeed, in management research, articles often use a mix of inclusive and exclusive argumentation to organize claims and substantiate contributions (Locke & Golden-Biddle, 1997;Weick, 1995).

Rhetorical tentativeness may indicate that the problematizing is indirect, incomplete, inadequate, or incommensurable, while rhetorical certainty may indicate that the problematizing is direct, complete, adequate, and commensurable (Alvesson & Sandberg, 2011;Locke & Golden-Biddle, 1997). A balance of tentativeness and certainty may help authors hedge by appropriately positioning and presenting their own contributions in a written text. paragraphs: The convictions of a thought collective are represented through the shared mission, vision, values, and codes of conduct of the association's members (Schnelle, 1981). A collective's distinctive thought style is aligned with these convictions and manifested in the conferences and journals that facilitate idea circulation and knowledge flow for the collective. Table 2 presents an overview of five prominent global thought collectives in management research.

To test our hypotheses, we must demarcate intellectual boundaries separating one collective from another. For example, as shown in Table 2, four of the five collectives have broad coverage of management research topics. Only the SMJ, the main publication of the Strategic Management Society (SMS), is constrained by its members to a single specialized area of management research-namely, strategy. Since our goal is to understand what drives knowledge consumption, we follow Nerur et al. (2016) and use the full-text archive of SMJ to clearly and conveniently identify knowledge content that a scientific community, the SMS, views as a body of management research on strategy topics. Thus, use of the SMJ archive clearly defines the SMS as our originating thought collective while providing a bounded corpus of text documents for the execution of this study. As noted in the introduction, there is a substantial bidirectional knowledge flow between articles published in SMJ and JOM, which indicates the direct relevance and suitability of the SMJ corpus for this study.

We accessed the SMJ's repository and downloaded all full-text articles published in the first 34 volumes . We explicitly excluded all editorial material, such as regular editorials and introductions to special issues, letters, book reviews, and research summaries. We augmented our text data with the corresponding citations and publication metadata from the WOS to confirm that we collected every article. This yielded 1,646 research articles with

The Strategic Management Society is unique in bringing together the worlds of reflective practice and thoughtful scholarship. The society focuses on the development and dissemination of insights on the strategic management process, as well as on fostering contacts and interchange around the world. We inspire and enable a better world through our scholarship and teaching about management and organizations. Not available Not available Mission We create value for our members, organizations, and society through professional development, high-impact scholarship and teaching, and engaging programs. We do this in a socially responsible manner while maintaining a sense of community that involves nurturing members, building collaborations, enhancing life-long friendships, and making a difference with our activities. The society operates exclusively for charitable, scientific, and educational purposes to promote and encourage superior research and practice in the field of strategic management. To build a vibrant and supportive community of scholars by markedly expanding opportunities to connect and explore ideas. INFORMS will lead in the development, dissemination and implementation of knowledge, basic and applied research and technologies in operations research, analytics, the management sciences, and related methods of improving operational processes, decision making, and management. The Academy of International Business is the leading global community of scholars for the creation and dissemination of knowledge about international business and policy issues. The academy transcends the boundaries of single academic disciplines and managerial functions to enhance business education and practice. (continued) Thought Collective Southern Management Association Strategic Management Society Academy of Management INFORMS Academy of International Business Values We will be good stewards of Southern Management Association resources.
We will develop opportunities for association member engagement.
We will engage in our activities in ways that enhance the reputation of the association.
We will enhance quality consistent with our vision and mission in all association activities.

sections.paper_id: Antons et al. - 2019 - Content, Contribution, and Knowledge Consumption Uncovering Hidden Topic Structure and Rhetorical S
paragraphs: Given our theoretical framework and the size of our sample, scalable methods are essential to capture our proposed topic and rhetorical attributes. Unlike prior research-which relies solely on manual coding of selective subsamples of written texts (e.g., Colquitt & Zapata-Phelan, 2007;Locke & Golden-Biddle, 1997) or small text snippets, such as keywords or headings (e.g., Bergh et al., 2006;Stremersch et al., 2007)-our analysis is based on the complete body of full text for an entire journal (1,646 SMJ articles and nearly 18 million words). To accomplish this, we build on recent developments in automated text analysis. Text-mining and natural language processing algorithms are quickly advancing in terms of computational efficiency and analytical capabilities, making them broadly useful for a range of social science research applications (O'Connor, Bamman, & Smith, 2011). In general, the advantage of using text mining for categorizing and classifying text documents is that it is rapid, replicable, and reliable (Bao & Datta, 2014). This is especially advantageous for extremely large corpora of unstructured text documents, where manual coding would be costly, time-consuming, and potentially susceptible to human coding errors or biases. In particular, we combine the latest algorithmic advances from computational linguistics, natural language processing, and statistical machine learning to analyze topic and rhetorical attributes. We utilize latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) to model and unearth hidden topic structure and automated content analysis to detect rhetorical signals.
We briefly describe both techniques here and in detail in Online Appendix 1.

sections.paper_id: Antons et al. - 2019 - Content, Contribution, and Knowledge Consumption Uncovering Hidden Topic Structure and Rhetorical S
paragraphs: LDA is the core of a larger family of topic modeling techniques (for an overview, see Blei, 2012) and is especially well suited to discovering latent topic structures. It enables researchers to identify previously unknown thematic clusters in extremely large sets of full-text documents, without assuming any prior knowledge of existing classification schemes (Bao & Datta, 2014;Blei, 2012). Hence, if there are hidden yet consistent patterns present in authors' written word choices beyond, for instance, explicit editorial policies about the use of certain keywords or terminology, these subtle patterns are detectable. Recent social science research successfully demonstrated the wide applicability of LDA in extracting articles' latent topic structure from scholarly journals' defined corpora in diverse fields ranging from literature and history to folklore (Goldstone & Underwood, 2014; Jockers & Mimno, 2013;Laudun & Goodwin, 2013). The latest studies in innovation (Antons & Breidbach, 2018;Antons, Kleer, & Salge, 2016) and marketing (X. S. Wang, Bendle, Mai, & Cotte, 2015) show how a journal's topic landscape evolves as new articles are published and discernible patterns of topics are formed. Applying topic modeling to identify breakthrough patents, scholars note that LDA is "vital in understanding the emergence and institutionalization of new fields" (S. Kaplan & Vakili, 2015: 1454). Blei, Ng, and Jordan (2003) introduced the LDA algorithm based on the assumption that authors who seek to address a certain topic use a specific vocabulary or lexicon that is associated with this topic. This is consistent with Astley's (1985) definition of scientific fields as "word systems" and Fleck's (1979) premise that a thought collective's distinctive thought style influences the word choices made by authors to express their ideas in the articles published in the collective's journals. In LDA, a text document may include different topics, each described by its own vocabulary. In line with this reasoning, each document in the collection of written texts of a given thought collective (i.e., a journal archive) is simply a bundle of topics. Thus, each document is characterized by its own topic distribution. Topics are reflected in the related words appearing in full text and are thereby measurable as a percentage of the entire content of the document. The validity of this distributional assumption and percentage representation is supported by multiple recent studies of various collections of research articles (Blei & Lafferty, 2009; Goldstone & Underwood, 2014; X. S. Wang et al., 2015). As Blei (2012) states, using an analogy, scholars may think of LDA as a principal component analysis for discrete text data-a technique applied for metric data in factor analysis with which management scholars may be familiar.

The LDA algorithm outputs two matrices. The first matrix contains a topic list and a corresponding vector of words that best represent each topic. The second matrix contains the topic list and all analyzed documents. These matrix values quantify how strongly associated each topic is with each document, via a loading or estimated weighting. A larger weighting value indicates that a given topic is more strongly associated with a particular document. Both matrices provide essential information on the journal's overall topic landscape and the topic structure of each article in the corpus. We use these data to quantify each article's relative concentration of topics and its degree of topic focus. We use an article's publication date to determine its relative order of entry into topics and measure its degree of topic newness. See Online Appendix 1 for technical details on LDA and our process for generating a topic model.

sections.header_raw: Extracting Rhetorical Attributes With Automated Content Analysis
sections.header_norm: extracting rhetorical attributes with automated content analysis
paragraphs: To identify connective discourse markers, we employ an automated dictionary-based content analysis technique that is widely established in the humanities and social sciences. This technique emerged from early attempts to extract linguistic features from text by means of customized dictionaries (Pennebaker & King, 1999). In psychology, such specialized dictionaries are used to detect markers of suicidal tendencies (Thomas & Duszynski, 1985) and depression (Schnurr, Rosenberg, & Oxman, 1992) expressed in patient communications. In management research, dictionary-based approaches are used to capture media response (Bednar, 2012), investor sentiment (Price, Doran, Peterson, & Bliss, 2012), time orientation (Nadkarni & Chen, 2014), and regulatory focus (Gamache, McNamara, Mannor, & Johnson, 2015) in an array of texts ranging from CEO speeches to earnings calls, annual reports, and newspaper articles.

Several software packages are available for automated dictionary-based content analyses. In this study, we used the Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count (LIWC) 2015 software (Pennebaker, Boyd, Jordan, & Blackburn, 2015), which includes 92 language categories composed of >6,400 words and word stems. As Tausczik and Pennebaker (2010: 35) explain, "thinking can vary in depth and complexity; this is reflected in the words people use to connect thoughts." LIWC's predefined dictionaries capture how people connect thoughts in spoken and written form. There are three main reasons why we used LIWC to extract the rhetorical signals represented by connective discourse markers. First, LIWC dictionaries have transparent lexicons that are available for examination. Second, the reliability of these dictionaries is established by extensive analyses of their psychometric properties developed via a comprehensive set of 181,000 text files, spanning five main corpora and totaling approximately 231 million words (Pennebaker, Boyd, et al., 2015). LIWC language categories are validated in hundreds of published studies (for an overview, see Tausczik & Pennebaker, 2010). Finally, the use of LIWC is increasingly accepted and was formally documented in recent empirical research in management (e.g., Bednar, 2012;Gamache et al., 2015;Zavyalova, Pfarrer, Reger, & Shapiro, 2012). We now describe how we used LIWC to measure the key rhetorical attributes in our corpus.

sections.paper_id: Antons et al. - 2019 - Content, Contribution, and Knowledge Consumption Uncovering Hidden Topic Structure and Rhetorical S
paragraphs: Dependent Variables Scientific impact. The number of forward citations that an article receives is the prevailing measure of scientific impact in management (Bergh et al., 2006;Slavova, Fosfuri, & De Castro, 2016) and beyond (King, 2004;Radicchi, Fortunato, & Castellano, 2008). Following this approach, we measure scientific impact as the average annual number of citations received as indexed in the WOS. 1 Unlike Google Scholar, this conservative measure of scientific impact captures only forward citations in peer-reviewed articles published in WOS-listed journals. These journals tend to be of higher quality than the broader set of outlets indexed by Google Scholar (X. S. Wang et al., 2015). However, as a robustness check, we replicated our findings with citation data from Google Scholar. Since citation data are usually highly skewed (Slovava et al., 2016), we took the square root to normalize the distribution (Judge et al., 2007). Hypotheses H7, H8a, and H8b pertain to expected differences in scientific impact between the focal thought collective and other related collectives. For these hypotheses, we distinguish among forward citations received from articles published in SMJ, JOM, Academy of Management Journal, Academy of Management Review, Management Science, Organization Science, and the Journal of International Business Studies. These journals are, arguably, the flagship journals of the management research thought collectives listed in Table 2. We downloaded the full records of all 172,237 forward citations of our entire corpus and calculated average annual citation counts per citing journal and SMJ article. Again, we used the square root to handle skewness.

sections.paper_id: Antons et al. - 2019 - Content, Contribution, and Knowledge Consumption Uncovering Hidden Topic Structure and Rhetorical S
paragraphs: Topic structure. To test our baseline hypothesis, H0, we included the loadings for all 83 topics computed with LDA to capture the latent topic structure of each article in our collection (see Online Appendix 1 for detailed information on running LDA). We replaced loadings below the cutoff of 0.1 for meaningful loadings by a value of zero (Antons et al., 2016;S. Kaplan & Vakili, 2015).

Topic focus. To measure an article's level of topic focus, we utilized a standard Herfindahl index (Hirschmann, 1964). We calculated the index based on the 83 topic structure variables that indicate an article's topic loadings on the overall topic landscape of our corpus.

Topic newness. Given the topic structure variables, we calculated the rank that an individual article has among other articles loading on the same topic with respect to the chronological order of publication. The publication order is measured at the level of a published journal issue. If multiple articles that load on the same topic appear in the same issue, these articles have the same rank. After assigning ranks, we calculated an article's minimum rank across all topics on which it loads. Because a lower rank corresponds to an earlier order of publication, we took the inverse of the minimum rank to compute newness. Hence, topic newness decreases the later that an article appears in the order of publication. Since reciprocal values of rank data are highly skewed, we use the logarithm function to obtain a more normal distribution.

Balance of inclusiveness and exclusiveness. To measure the balance of inclusiveness and exclusiveness, we relied on LIWC. We used the LIWC category conjunctions to measure inclusive rhetoric in texts as the percentage of words in this category. The category includes 43 words, such as and, both, and not only. Conjunctions connect claims and create coherent narratives (Graesser, McNamara, Louwerse, & Cai, 2004). For example, prior work uses this category to capture how the U.S. Federal Reserve chair integrates multiple streams of thought in the text of economic policy speeches (Abe, 2011). To measure exclusive rhetoric in texts, we used the LIWC category differentiation, which includes 81 words, such as but, else, and nor. Differentiation terms compare competing claims and define contrasts (Tausczik & Pennebaker, 2010). These words help authors to "distinguish between entities, people, or ideas" (Pennebaker, Booth, et al., 2015: 22). For instance, previous research uses differentiation to compare citizens' and leaders' "differences of opinion, belief, values, goals, or world-views" (Murray, Xu, & Woolf, 2013: 1). 2 We follow prior research on exploration and exploitation that captures the balance of the two constructs via their absolute difference (He & Wong, 2004). Hence, we measured the balance of inclusiveness and exclusiveness as the difference of the LIWC categories conjunctions and differentiation. Accordingly, the variable has a range of -1 to 1, with 0 indicating a fully balanced rhetorical style. To test H3a, we tested for an inverted U-shape.

paragraphs: To measure the balance of tentativeness and certainty, we followed a similar logic as stated earlier. The LIWC category tentativeness measures tentative rhetoric in texts as the percentage of tentative words. This category includes 178 words, such as maybe, might, and perhaps. These words may signal cautious, preliminary claims that invite future inquiry (Pennebaker, Boyd, et al., 2015). For example, prior studies use tentativeness to assess the online discourse associated with collaborative problemsolving activities (Joksimovic, Gasevic, Kovanovic, Adesope, & Hatala, 2014). The LIWC category certainty measures rhetorical certainty in texts. This category includes 113 words, such as always, definitely, and never. These words may signal assured, definitive claims that limit future inquiry (Pennebaker & King, 1999). For instance, previous work shows that more certain phrasing in U.S. Supreme Court decisions affects the way that lower courts notice and follow the higher court's precedents (Corley & Wedeking, 2014). 3 Again, we measure the balance of tentativeness and certainty as the difference of the LIWC categories tentativeness and certainty. As above, a value of 0 indicates a fully balanced rhetorical style. To test H3b, we test for an inverted U-shape.

paragraphs: We broadly control for alternative factors to isolate the effects of topic and rhetorical attributes from the possibly confounding effects of author, reference, and linguistic attributes on citations.

Author attributes. First, we control for the count of authors that may affect scientific impact (Bergh et al., 2006;Li, Liao, & Yen, 2013). Second, as prior experience and status within a thought collective might influence the likelihood of publishing another article in the focal journal and create more awareness in the collective, we control for authors' average prior experience (Bergh et al., 2006). We do so by calculating the average number of articles that each author published in our corpus, prior to the focal article. Third, we control for author prominence within the management domain (Judge et al., 2007;Li et al., 2013), as reflected in the average citations of all articles that the respective author coauthored and published in the top management journals. 4 For multiauthor articles, we sum the individual author prominence values. Fourth, we include dummy variables with the school's research rank to account for author affiliation (Judge et al., 2007;Li et al., 2013;Long, Bowers, Barnett, & White, 1998). 5 Reference attributes. We include the count of references, or total number of backward citations contained in an article (Judge et al., 2007). Linguistic attributes. We control for language complexity as a driver of impact (Judge et al., 2007;Whissell, 1999). We use the automated readability index (ARI) indicating the U.S. school grade required to understand a given text (Kane & Ransbotham, 2016;Smith & Senter, 1967). It is calculated as ARI = (4.71 × letters/words) + (0.5 × words/sentences) -21.43. We use the "tm package" for the statistical software package R to compute the ARI. We also measure article length. While prior bibliometric studies use page counts (Acedo, Barroso, Casanueva, & Galán, 2006;Judge et al., 2007;Trieschmann, Dennis, Northcraft, & Niemi, 2000), we use a word count instead, since the exact level of analysis in LDA and LIWC 2015 is the word.
Other attribute. We account for articles that are part of a special issue by including a dummy variable (coded 1 for a special issue and 0 otherwise; Bergh et al., 2006). 6

Fourth, we include dummy variables with the school's research rank to account for author affiliation (Judge et al., 2007;Li et al., 2013;Long, Bowers, Barnett, & White, 1998). 5 Reference attributes. We include the count of references, or total number of backward citations contained in an article (Judge et al., 2007). Linguistic attributes. We control for language complexity as a driver of impact (Judge et al., 2007;Whissell, 1999). We use the automated readability index (ARI) indicating the U.S. school grade required to understand a given text (Kane & Ransbotham, 2016;Smith & Senter, 1967). It is calculated as ARI = (4.71 × letters/words) + (0.5 × words/sentences) -21.43. We use the "tm package" for the statistical software package R to compute the ARI. We also measure article length. While prior bibliometric studies use page counts (Acedo, Barroso, Casanueva, & Galán, 2006;Judge et al., 2007;Trieschmann, Dennis, Northcraft, & Niemi, 2000), we use a word count instead, since the exact level of analysis in LDA and LIWC 2015 is the word.

Other attribute. We account for articles that are part of a special issue by including a dummy variable (coded 1 for a special issue and 0 otherwise; Bergh et al., 2006). 6

paragraphs: Since normalizing our dependent variable(s) addresses the skewness issue and we measure variables on an interval scale, ordinary least squares regressions with robust standard errors provide efficient estimates for our hypothesis testing. We checked for multicollinearity using variance inflation factors (all were <3.50 and the condition number was 14.97), indicating no issues. We standardized all variables before using STATA 13.1 to run our analyses.

We present the regression results for H0 to H6 in Table 4. Model 1 serves as the baseline model containing controls only. Model 2 adds topic structure to the analysis (we do not report the single coefficients for all 83 variables here; they are available in Online Appendix 2). The topic attributes topic focus and topic newness are added in Models 3 and 4. We add the variables to measure the balance of rhetorical attributes sequentially in Models 5 to 7. Models 8 and 10 add the interaction terms of topic focus and topic newness and between topic newness and knowledge inflow. The interaction terms of topic newness and the balances of rhetorical attributes are introduced in Models 11 and 12. Model 13 is the fully specified model from which we extract all standardized coefficient estimates and effect sizes presented here.

As for the controls, we see that, similar to the correlations reported earlier, author prominence (b = 0.47, p < .01) and word count (b = 0.14, p < .01) show positive and significant coefficients. Moreover, articles published by authors affiliated with schools in the top tier of the Financial Times ranking generate higher scientific impact (b = 0.28, p < .01). Language complexity, in turn, shows a negative and significant coefficient (b = -0.07, p < .05), indicating that greater readability increases subsequent impact. While adding author prominence has the strongest effect among the control variables, increasing the adjusted coefficient of determination by 0.10, we see that adding topic structure to the model increases the adjusted coefficient of determination by 0.14. We also find that the 83 topics differ in their impact, thus providing evidence for our baseline hypothesis (H0).

We find support for H1 that greater topic focus is associated with higher scientific impact, since the coefficient estimate is positive and significant (b = 0.23, p < .01). Since all variables are standardized, the coefficient directly tells us the magnitude of the effect. A 1-SD increase in topic focus is associated with a predicted increase in scientific impact of 0.23. Retransforming the dependent variable to its original metric shows a citation premium per SD increase in topic focus of 1.03 additional annual citations (+22.9%) relative to an article at the mean of topic focus.

In H2, we expect that a greater degree of topic newness is related to higher scientific impact. Our analysis provides support for this relationship (b = 0.30, p < .01). A 1-SD increase in topic newness is associated with a citation premium of 1.36 additional annual citations (+30.3%). Thus, we find support for H2.

Regarding the question of whether balancing rhetorical signals is associated with scientific impact, we find no significant effects for the balance of tentativeness and certainty (b = 0.04, ns) and the respective squared term testing the inverted U-shape (b = 0.00, ns). In turn, we find a significant effect for the balance of inclusiveness and exclusiveness (b = 0.13, p < .01) and the respective squared term (b = 0.04, p < .05). However, we do not find support for the hypothesized effect of balancing the two rhetorical attributes. Figure 1 illustrates the nonlinear effect. As shown, we do not find an inverted U-shape, which would have indicated that balanced rhetoric is linked to greater scientific impact. Instead, we find that being more inclusive than exclusive is linked to greater impact. Thus, we do not find support for H3a and H3b.

H4 predicted a positive interaction effect of topic focus and topic newness, which we find in the results (b = 0.11, p < .01). To ease interpretation, we plot the results in Figure 2. As shown, topic focus amplifies the relationship of topic newness and scientific impact, especially for high degrees of newness and focus. This is further supported by a simple slope test of the regression lines regarding all possible combinations of low (1 SD below the mean) and high (1 SD above the mean) levels of the independent and the moderator variables (Dawson, 2014). The simple slopes for both topic newness with low (t = 3.11, p < .01) and high (t = 4.92, p < .001) topic focus are significantly different from zero. The gradient (b = 0.412) is stronger for high than for low (b = 0.196) topic focus, supporting the interaction plot results. Regarding magnitude, the positive interaction means that articles with a 1-SD increase in both topic attributes receive a premium of 3.07 additional annual citations (+68.3%) relative to articles at the mean of topic newness and topic focus. Thus, we find support for H4.

In H2, we expect that a greater degree of topic newness is related to higher scientific impact. Our analysis provides support for this relationship (b = 0.30, p < .01). A 1-SD increase in topic newness is associated with a citation premium of 1.36 additional annual citations (+30.3%). Thus, we find support for H2.

Regarding the question of whether balancing rhetorical signals is associated with scientific impact, we find no significant effects for the balance of tentativeness and certainty (b = 0.04, ns) and the respective squared term testing the inverted U-shape (b = 0.00, ns). In turn, we find a significant effect for the balance of inclusiveness and exclusiveness (b = 0.13, p < .01) and the respective squared term (b = 0.04, p < .05). However, we do not find support for the hypothesized effect of balancing the two rhetorical attributes. Figure 1 illustrates the nonlinear effect. As shown, we do not find an inverted U-shape, which would have indicated that balanced rhetoric is linked to greater scientific impact. Instead, we find that being more inclusive than exclusive is linked to greater impact. Thus, we do not find support for H3a and H3b.

H4 predicted a positive interaction effect of topic focus and topic newness, which we find in the results (b = 0.11, p < .01). To ease interpretation, we plot the results in Figure 2. As shown, topic focus amplifies the relationship of topic newness and scientific impact, especially for high degrees of newness and focus. This is further supported by a simple slope test of the regression lines regarding all possible combinations of low (1 SD below the mean) and high (1 SD above the mean) levels of the independent and the moderator variables (Dawson, 2014). The simple slopes for both topic newness with low (t = 3.11, p < .01) and high (t = 4.92, p < .001) topic focus are significantly different from zero. The gradient (b = 0.412) is stronger for high than for low (b = 0.196) topic focus, supporting the interaction plot results. Regarding magnitude, the positive interaction means that articles with a 1-SD increase in both topic attributes receive a premium of 3.07 additional annual citations (+68.3%) relative to articles at the mean of topic newness and topic focus. Thus, we find support for H4.
sections.preview_head: Since normalizing our dependent variable(s) addresses the skewness issue and we measure variables on an interval scale, ordinary least squares regressions with robust standard errors provide efficient estimates for our hypothesis testing. We checked for multicollinearity using variance inflation factors (all were <3.50 and the condition number was 14.97), indicating no issues. We standardized all variables before using STATA 13.1 to run our analyses.

paragraphs: The interaction term between topic newness and knowledge inflow is positive and significant (b = 0.10, p < .10). Figure 3 shows the corresponding interaction plot. This illustrates that knowledge inflow reinforces the positive association between topic newness and scientific impact, especially when knowledge inflow and topic newness are both high. While both simple slopes for topic newness with low (t = 3.94, p < .001) and high (t = 4.00, p < .001) knowledge inflow are significantly different from zero, the gradient is greater for high (b = 0.400) than for low (b = 0.208) knowledge inflow, supporting the finding of the interaction plot. A 1-SD increase in both topic characteristics is thus associated with a citation premium of 0.93 additional annual citations (+20.8%). Hence, we find support for H5.

For the interaction terms of topic newness and the balances between the pairs of rhetorical attributes, we find no significant interaction for the balance of inclusiveness and exclusiveness (b = 0.05, ns) and the respective squared term (b = 0.02, ns). In contrast, we find a Figure 3 Interaction Effect of Topic Newness and Knowledge Inflow

significant interaction between topic newness and the balance of tentativeness and certainty (b = 0.08, p < .01) and the respective squared term (b = -0.03, p < .01). Figure 4 illustrates that if the absolute difference between tentativeness and certainty increases, scientific impact is greater when topic newness is high. This means that low tentativeness and high certainty as a rhetorical style are less effective in establishing a scientific contribution than being more tentative and less certain. Interestingly, the curve for low topic newness exhibits a different pattern. Its U-shape implies that being balanced (tentativeness and certainty show similar values) is less effective in establishing contribution than being cautious (highly tentative and less certain) or dogmatic (highly certain and less tentative). Thus, we do not find support for H6a and H6b.

The regression results for H7 and H8 are reported in Table 5. We replicate the full models including all terms described so far for scientific impact in different thought collectives (i.e., journals). Regarding H7, we find significant effects for topic focus across all models except for Model 20, testing the effect on citations in the Journal of International Business Studies. The coefficient difference tests reveal that the regression coefficient for topic focus in Model 14 (SMJ as focal thought collective) is significantly stronger than for all other models. Thus, we find support for H7.

Regarding topic newness, we find significant effects of newness across Models 14 to 20. As for the difference in the effect of topic newness on scientific impact within the focal thought collective and in other collectives (H8a), we find significant effects in all coefficient difference tests, indicating that the effects are stronger inside the focal thought collective than outside. Thus, we find strong support for H8a. The interaction effect of topic newness and knowledge inflow is positive and significant in all models except for the Academy of Management Review and Management Science. This indicates that success at translating novel topics to another thought collective is generally increased when drawing on intellectual content from the respective target collective. Thus, we find partial support for H8b.

As summarized in Table 6, our empirical results provide broad support for all of our hypotheses except H3a, H3b, H6a, and H6b. The interaction term between topic newness and knowledge inflow is positive and significant (b = 0.10, p < .10). Figure 3 shows the corresponding interaction plot. This illustrates that knowledge inflow reinforces the positive association between topic newness and scientific impact, especially when knowledge inflow and topic newness are both high. While both simple slopes for topic newness with low (t = 3.94, p < .001) and high (t = 4.00, p < .001) knowledge inflow are significantly different from zero, the gradient is greater for high (b = 0.400) than for low (b = 0.208) knowledge inflow, supporting the finding of the interaction plot. A 1-SD increase in both topic characteristics is thus associated with a citation premium of 0.93 additional annual citations (+20.8%). Hence, we find support for H5.

For the interaction terms of topic newness and the balances between the pairs of rhetorical attributes, we find no significant interaction for the balance of inclusiveness and exclusiveness (b = 0.05, ns) and the respective squared term (b = 0.02, ns). In contrast, we find a Figure 3 Interaction Effect of Topic Newness and Knowledge Inflow

significant interaction between topic newness and the balance of tentativeness and certainty (b = 0.08, p < .01) and the respective squared term (b = -0.03, p < .01). Figure 4 illustrates that if the absolute difference between tentativeness and certainty increases, scientific impact is greater when topic newness is high. This means that low tentativeness and high certainty as a rhetorical style are less effective in establishing a scientific contribution than being more tentative and less certain. Interestingly, the curve for low topic newness exhibits a different pattern. Its U-shape implies that being balanced (tentativeness and certainty show similar values) is less effective in establishing contribution than being cautious (highly tentative and less certain) or dogmatic (highly certain and less tentative). Thus, we do not find support for H6a and H6b.

The regression results for H7 and H8 are reported in Table 5. We replicate the full models including all terms described so far for scientific impact in different thought collectives (i.e., journals). Regarding H7, we find significant effects for topic focus across all models except for Model 20, testing the effect on citations in the Journal of International Business Studies. The coefficient difference tests reveal that the regression coefficient for topic focus in Model 14 (SMJ as focal thought collective) is significantly stronger than for all other models. Thus, we find support for H7.

Regarding topic newness, we find significant effects of newness across Models 14 to 20. As for the difference in the effect of topic newness on scientific impact within the focal thought collective and in other collectives (H8a), we find significant effects in all coefficient difference tests, indicating that the effects are stronger inside the focal thought collective than outside. Thus, we find strong support for H8a. The interaction effect of topic newness and knowledge inflow is positive and significant in all models except for the Academy of Management Review and Management Science. This indicates that success at translating novel topics to another thought collective is generally increased when drawing on intellectual content from the respective target collective. Thus, we find partial support for H8b.

As summarized in Table 6, our empirical results provide broad support for all of our hypotheses except H3a, H3b, H6a, and H6b. sections.header_raw: Robustness Checks and Post Hoc Findings
sections.header_norm: robustness checks and post hoc findings
paragraphs: We conducted a number of additional analyses to test the robustness of our findings and explore emerging questions. First, to overcome possible concerns due to the transformation of our dependent variable, we adopted an alternative approach to normalize the distribution of the average citations per year, using a logarithmic instead of a square root transformation. Second, some articles received no citations at all, which leads to an average of zero annual citations, resulting in a censored dependent variable. We thus replicated our analyses using a Tobit estimator. Third, we replicated our analyses using citation data extracted manually from Google Scholar. The results of the different regression models for all robustness checks are available in Online Appendix 3. As shown there, the effects are robust to changes of the transformation technique, the regression estimator employed, and the measurement of the dependent variable. Fourth, the results are also robust to the inclusion of the publication year as shown in Model 5. Fifth, to delve more deeply into the effect of topic newness, we replicate Model 13 and include a nonlinear (i.e., squared) term. The corresponding model is in Online Appendix 3 as Model 6. The negative and significant coefficient of the squared term indicates an inverted U-shaped relationship between topic newness and scientific impact (b = -0.09, p < .01). We illustrate the curvilinear effect in Figure 5. Here, we retransformed the variables for an easier interpretation of the effect. As shown in Figure 5, scientific impact, on average, peaks for the first and second articles published on a novel topic. Compared with the mean scientific impact of articles with topic newness Rank 14 and 3.79 annual citations, the first article on each topic receives 8.17 annual citations (+115.6%). The second article exhibits a marginally less strong effect, with an average of 7.87 annual citations (+107.7%), while the third paper receives 6.77 citations (+78.6%).

sections.paper_id: Antons et al. - 2019 - Content, Contribution, and Knowledge Consumption Uncovering Hidden Topic Structure and Rhetorical S
paragraphs: We propose topic structure and rhetorical signals as highly salient yet underexplored attributes that drive intra-and intercollective knowledge consumption above and beyond the established effects of author, reference, journal, and linguistic attributes. We employ LDA and LIWC as two automated text analysis techniques to extract topic and rhetorical attributes, respectively, from a large full-text corpus of management research. Applying these techniques, our empirical analyses uncover how both attribute sets-independently and in concert-relate to scientific impact across flagship management journals. As such, our study may be among the first large-scale empirical tests of Fleck's (1979) theory of thought collectives and thought styles as a plausible explanation for the effects of latent topic structure and rhetorical signals on scientific impact. We discuss the implications of our research on scientific knowledge consumption and for the members and leaders of thought collectives.

sections.paper_id: Antons et al. - 2019 - Content, Contribution, and Knowledge Consumption Uncovering Hidden Topic Structure and Rhetorical S
sections.header_raw: Implications for Research on Knowledge Consumption
sections.header_norm: implications for research on knowledge consumption
paragraphs: Our work offers a deeper investigation of the essential yet often hidden and overlooked role of topic and rhetorical attributes in management research. We provide explanations and evidence for how knowledge is consumed in management research. Here, we offer three initial, interrelated contributions: (1) a novel conceptual framework linking scientific discourse to the topics and rhetoric of written texts; (2) a generalizable and scalable text analysis methodology to detect and measure topic structure, focus, and newness as well as four distinct rhetorical signals; and (3) a demonstration of the value of this framework and methodology for empirically analyzing the scientific impact of a defined thought collective with a bounded corpus.
