sections.paper_id: Antons et al. - 2019 - Content, Contribution, and Knowledge Consumption Uncovering Hidden Topic Structure and Rhetorical S
sections.header_raw: Figure 5 Illustration of Nonlinear Effect of Topic Newness
sections.header_norm: figure 5 illustration of nonlinear effect of topic newness
paragraphs: Note: We retransformed the newness ranks as well as the predicted impact for an easier interpretation.

First, our study builds on Fleck's (1979) original concepts of thought collectives and thought styles as well as the notion of scientific fields as word systems to provide a framework designed to illuminate studies on the production and consumption of knowledge content in science and other domains. We extend prior work in a new direction by providing a theoretical basis for explaining how a text's thematic substance and argumentation style, embodied in salient topic and rhetorical attributes, meaningfully indicate future knowledge consumption. Specifically, we account for the circulation of ideas within and among thought collectives and elaborate on thought styles as the critical element by which these ideas become embedded in a journal article's published text. Perhaps most important, we provide the foundation for conceptualizing scholarly articles and other outputs of scientific knowledge production (e.g., grant or patent applications) as topic bundles, each represented by a unique word vector. Topic structure, focus, and newness then emerge as key aspects of the topic distribution that describes such topic bundles. Finally, we show that rhetoric connecting claims intra-and intertextually relates to knowledge consumption directly and in concert with topic newness.

Second, our conceptual framework led us to develop an efficient and scalable methodology that leverages recent advances in topic modeling algorithms to unpack large text corpora that are not conducive to manual coding. By doing so, we begin to answer long existing calls for new methods to quantify the content of scholarly articles (McKinley, Mone, & Moon, 1999). Specifically, we introduce a standardized analytical procedure and associated measures to unearth the latent topic landscape of any text corpus (e.g., journal, journal basket, individual or organizational article portfolio) and describe the underlying topic distribution (i.e., topic structure, focus, and newness) of each constitutive entity. This methodology is generalizable to a broad set of texts, including scientific research (e.g., scholarly articles, grant proposals, patent applications) and other documents that regularly inform management research (e.g., advertisements, annual reports, court transcripts, press announcements, press articles). Although not without their own limitations, our procedure and measures may facilitate systematic large-scale topic analysis to reveal new insights about the substance and style of written texts.

Third, we demonstrate the value of our integrated framework and methodology by applying it to reveal the hidden role of topic attributes in shaping knowledge consumption in management research. Our regression analyses indicate that topic newness is indeed valued and cited in management research. Perhaps more intriguing, our post hoc analyses reveal that topic newness exhibits a pronounced nonlinear effect. Our results indicate that, in particular, the first two papers on a new topic generate the most citations. This implies that citation premiums result not only from originating a new topic but also from amplifying, expanding, and translating the topic. Moreover, our analyses reveal that to diffuse a new topic intercollectively, authors are advised to interpose it into ongoing debates in a focal scientific community (i.e., a thought collective). By thoughtfully connecting new topics to existing topics and ongoing discussions that are already familiar to members of the target collective, authors may translate their article's content into a more widely consumable form and thereby enrich the research in their field. Our findings on a nonlinear newness effect at the article level as well as the importance of translating new topics to fellow scholars might inform a spirited debate on the perceived value of and proper incentives for newness in research.

sections.paper_id: Antons et al. - 2019 - Content, Contribution, and Knowledge Consumption Uncovering Hidden Topic Structure and Rhetorical S
sections.header_raw: Implications for Members and Leaders of Scientific Thought Collectives
sections.header_norm: implications for members and leaders of scientific thought collectives
paragraphs: Our research shows that an article's thematic substance, as reflected in topic attributes, is perhaps the most important determinant of knowledge consumption and, therefore, a prime catalyst for future knowledge production. For authors, our research informs scholars how their choice of research topics and argumentation style may make their research more or less appealing to others, which affects its diffusion and consumption. We offer two main implications.

First, since scholars frequently face information overload (McKinley et al., 1999), attracting attention becomes crucial. Our empirical evidence suggests that an effective means of attracting attention is to sharply focus an article on a concentrated core topic that is clearly linked to inflows of existing research from the originating collective or other relevant collectives. Increasing focus makes the article easier to absorb, while strengthening knowledge inflows from existing research and using inclusive discourse markers reduce translation problems within and across collectives. Topic focus and knowledge inflow are associated with the accessibility of scientific texts and thereby affect the future consumption of these texts (Judge et al., 2007).

Second, while leading journals strive for new research (Colquitt & Zapata-Phelan, 2007), submitting new research ideas to these journals is a risky endeavor that may be daunting, especially for less prominent authors who may be lacking in social capital (Li et al., 2013;Starbuck, 2005). Our empirical evidence on the nonlinear effect of topic newness suggests that quickly amplifying and expanding the recent contributions of more prominent authors offers an attractive risk-reward trade-off. There appears to be a similar payoff in terms of citation premium for rapidly advancing a newly introduced research stream as compared with originating the stream.

sections.paper_id: Antons et al. - 2019 - Content, Contribution, and Knowledge Consumption Uncovering Hidden Topic Structure and Rhetorical S
paragraphs: Our proposed text mining methodology and measures have limitations that offer opportunities for future research. First and most important, LDA and, indeed, any type of algorithmic content analysis rely on simplifying assumptions and human interpretation, which makes the reflective use of such approaches all the more critical. LDA, for instance, is unable to extract obscure and infrequently discussed topics that appear only once or twice across the whole text corpus, with uncertainty around whether these topics will establish a new research stream or fade away (McKinley et al., 1999). We would hence like to recommend that future users of automated content analysis techniques follow four key principles that were valuable guideposts for this study (Grimmer & Stewart 2013): (1) all quantitative models of language are limited in their accuracy, but some are useful in generating insights; (2) models are meant to amplify and augment, not replace the "gold standard" of human reading and interpretation;
(3) there is no universal best modeling approach; and (4) model outputs should be validated.

A major implication of these principles is that quantitative models should be evaluated according to their ability to perform some useful social scientific task, such as assisting in discovering new text categorization schemes or extracting theoretically relevant metrics from large corpora (Grimmer & Stewart, 2013). Another important implication is that researchers need to be actively involved in making decisions and setting parameters to guide the process and to do so with the recognition that "establishing one method to always use for one task is almost guaranteed to be impossible" (Grimmer & Stewart, 2013: 271). A further implication is that validation may take considerable time and require substantial qualitative analysis conducted by human coders. We emphasize these principles and their implications to strongly encourage future researchers to exercise appropriate care and thoughtful caution when applying techniques such as LDA to the analyses of corpora. We explicitly discourage the blind use of quantitative text analysis without first presenting a plausible theoretical basis for the proposed causal mechanism under study.

With the principles and implications clearly delineated, we would like to highlight that LDA forces topics to be uncorrelated, nonhierarchical, and time independent. We chose this form of LDA to generate a manageable number of topics with a simplified topic structure and a static time frame to facilitate ease of implementation and reduce the computational resources required. However, other variants of LDA and other types of automated content analysis algorithms exist that may address some of these limitations (see Online Appendix 1). Following the four guiding principles that we delineated earlier, scholars may seek to explore alternative techniques suitable for investigating other large collections of texts, including books, patents, trial transcripts, ads and social media data, newspaper and annual reports, and grant applications.

A second major limitation is that our study relies on citation data to assess scientific impact. Although this is common in studies on knowledge production and consumption (e.g., Baldi, 1998), citations might be driven strategically (e.g., citing potential reviewers) and may not necessarily indicate a meaningful knowledge transfer between articles (Stremersch et al., 2007). Moreover, all citations are weighted equally in citation counts irrespective of how important they are to the citing article or whether they even negate the cited reference (Colquitt & Zapata-Phelan, 2007). Future studies should therefore attempt to unpack the nature of each forward citation received or investigate alternative measures of scientific impact, such as press attention to scientific articles, educational attention in textbooks and teaching materials, and the download statistics of research articles (Stremersch et al., 2007). In terms of backward citations, or knowledge inflow, recent work suggests that explorative articles incorporating novel combinations of referenced journals are significantly more likely to be highly cited across all scientific disciplines (J. Wang, Veugelers, & Stephan, 2017). In addition, all of our analyses are based on the corpus for one originating thought collective. This limits the generalizability of our empirical findings to a single specialized scientific community within the field of management that may not be fully representative of management research as a whole. However, our analyses show that many substantive conclusions hold across a broader set of flagship management journals. Future studies might further extend our work to analyze a larger body of management texts across a wider range of thought collectives.

Future studies should therefore attempt to unpack the nature of each forward citation received or investigate alternative measures of scientific impact, such as press attention to scientific articles, educational attention in textbooks and teaching materials, and the download statistics of research articles (Stremersch et al., 2007). In terms of backward citations, or knowledge inflow, recent work suggests that explorative articles incorporating novel combinations of referenced journals are significantly more likely to be highly cited across all scientific disciplines (J. Wang, Veugelers, & Stephan, 2017). In addition, all of our analyses are based on the corpus for one originating thought collective. This limits the generalizability of our empirical findings to a single specialized scientific community within the field of management that may not be fully representative of management research as a whole. However, our analyses show that many substantive conclusions hold across a broader set of flagship management journals. Future studies might further extend our work to analyze a larger body of management texts across a wider range of thought collectives.
sections.preview_head: Our proposed text mining methodology and measures have limitations that offer opportunities for future research. First and most important, LDA and, indeed, any type of algorithmic content analysis rely on simplifying assumptions and human interpretation, which makes the reflective use of such approaches all the more critical. LDA, for instance, is unable to extract obscure and infrequently discussed topics that appear only once or twice across the whole text corpus, with uncertainty around whether these topics will establish a new research stream or fade away (McKinley et al., 1999). We would hence like to recommend that future users of automated content analysis techniques follow four key principles that were valuable guideposts for this study (Grimmer & Stewart 2013): (1) all quantitative models of language are limited in their accuracy, but some are useful in generating insights; (2) models are meant to amplify and augment, not replace the "gold standard" of human reading and interpretation;
